text: Nothing entices me to literate programming more than blogging about code. :)

elements.text: Nothing entices me to literate programming more than blogging about code

text: I periodically noodle about a LP setup atop LÖVE. Nothing has come of it yet. The potential hot take here is that the need for LP is proportional to how static the language is and how large the codebase is. If you're in the right quadrant along those axes, you don't need it.

elements.text: I periodically noodle about a LP setup atop LÖVE. Nothing has come of it yet. The potential hot take here is that the need for LP is proportional to how static the language is and how large the codebase is. If you're in the right quadrant along those axes, you don't need it.

text: There’s a right amount of dynamism for not needing LP?

elements.text: There’s a right amount of dynamism for not needing LP?

text:
Dynamism isn't quite it. Maybe just sophistication? Consider Haskell. I'm not an expert, but I think it's sufficiently loosely coupled that you can get surprisingly far just by reordering definitions.

This is not to say we don't need LP in Haskell. We just don't need LP tools, Lhs notwithstanding. Forget the tools, focus on the work of describing codebases.

The other example I'm thinking of is JavaScript. Docco is a pretty nice tool. Even though it's not really LP, it's possible JavaScript needs nothing more. You can compose behaviors just using first class functions.
